name: Short tadalafil exposure worsened oxidative and muscle-damage markers after exhaustive exercise
statement: In healthy males, tadalafil exposure before exhaustive exercise decreased antioxidant capacity at rest and produced higher oxidative-damage and muscle-damage marker responses than placebo after exercise.
time_horizon: Two tadalafil doses before exercise with recovery sampling up to 60 minutes.
quantitative_note: Tadalafil per se changed GSH/GSSG -36%, CK +75%, LDH +36%, MDA +41%, and protein carbonyls +50% (all P<0.05); exercise further increased these biomarkers, with higher values in the tadalafil group versus placebo.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Abstract reports biomarker changes and conclusion that tadalafil made subjects more susceptible to exhaustive-exercise oxidative stress.

caveats: Clinical injury, soreness, and training adaptation were not established.
Sample size not extracted from abstract.
population_profile: Healthy males; relevant to safety/recovery hypotheses for active users.
tadalafil_exposure: Tadalafil 20 mg/day given as two tablets separated by 36 hours before exhaustive exercise.
indication_context: Experimental exercise recovery and oxidative-stress physiology.
endpoint_measure: GSH/GSSG, CK, LDH, MDA, protein carbonyls, IL-6, antioxidant status after exhaustive exercise.
effect_estimate: Biomarker profile favored more oxidative/muscle-damage signal with tadalafil, not recovery benefit.
safety_findings: Potential caution signal for strenuous/exhaustive exercise contexts; clinical event rates not reported.
directness_to_target_population: Moderate for healthy active men under exhaustive exercise, low for clinical injury risk or everyday workouts.
confounding_and_bias_limits: Biomarker endpoints, short time horizon, abstract-only sample details, and no direct performance/recovery outcome.
class_transfer_limits: Direct tadalafil evidence, but high acute dosing and exhaustive protocol limit generalization.
